en.extract:
The palm warbler (Setophaga palmarum) is a small songbird in the New World warbler family.


Description
Measurements:

Length: 12–14 cm (4.7–5.5 in)
Wingspan: 20–21 cm (7.9–8.3 in)
Weight: 8.5–14.2 g (0.30–0.50 oz)


Taxonomy
The species comprises two subspecies, with fairly extensive intergradation and mixing where they meet.

S. p. palmarum ("brown palm warbler" or "western palm warbler") inhabits the western two-thirds of the breeding range, and migrates southeast to winter on the Atlantic coast from Delaware to Florida (with a few on the Gulf coast west to Louisiana). It has much less yellow below, with less colorful streaking, and cold grayish-brown upper parts.
S. p. hypochrysea ("yellow palm warbler" or "eastern palm warbler") of the eastern third of the breeding range, and migrates southwest to winter on the Gulf coast from western Florida to Texas; it migrates later in the fall than its western counterpart. It has brownish-olive upper parts and thoroughly yellow underparts with bold rufous breast and flank streaking.


Distribution
Palm warblers breed in open coniferous bogs and edge east of the Continental Divide, across Canada and the northeastern United States.
These birds migrate to the southeastern United States, the Yucatán Peninsula, islands of the Caribbean, and eastern Nicaragua south to Panama to winter. They are one of the earlier migrants to return to their breeding grounds in the spring, often completing their migration almost two months before most other warblers. Unlike most Setophaga species, the palm warbler's winter range includes much of the Atlantic coast of North America, extending as far north as southern Nova Scotia. Every year since 1900 the palm warbler has been observed during Christmas Bird Count activities in Massachusetts, and consistently since 1958 in Nova Scotia. For the interval 1966–2015 the palm warbler population increased throughout much of its northernmost breeding range.
The palm warbler has been recorded as a vagrant to Iceland.


Behavior

Palm warbler nests take the form of an open cup, usually situated on or near the ground in an open area.
Palm warblers forage on the ground much more than other warblers, sometimes flying to catch insects. These birds mainly eat insects and berries. Their constant tail bobbing is an identifying characteristic. Kirtland's, prairie, and palm warblers are the only Setophaga species that incessantly bob their tails.
The song of this bird is a monotonous buzzy trill. The call is a sharp chek.


Gallery


References


External links

Palm warbler - Dendroica palmarum Archived 2010-05-31 at the Wayback Machine - USGS Patuxent Bird Identification InfoCenter
Palm warbler species account - Cornell Lab of Ornithology
Stamps (for British Virgin Islands) at bird-stamps.org
"Palm warbler media". Internet Bird Collection.
Palm Warbler photo gallery at VIREO (Drexel University)

The '''palm warbler''' ('''''Setophaga palmarum''''') is a small [[songbird]] in the [[New World warbler]] family.

== Description ==
'''Measurements''':<ref>{{Cite web|title=Palm Warbler Identification, All About Birds, Cornell Lab of Ornithology|url=https://www.allaboutbirds.org/guide/Palm_Warbler/id|access-date=2020-09-30|website=www.allaboutbirds.org|language=en}}</ref>

* '''Length''': {{cvt|12|-|14|cm|in}}
* '''Wingspan''': {{cvt|20|-|21|cm|in}}
* '''Weight''': {{cvt|8.5|–|14.2|g|oz}}

== Taxonomy ==
The species comprises two subspecies,<ref name="IOC">{{cite web | title=New World warblers, mitrospingid tanagers – IOC World Bird List | website=IOC World Bird List – Version 14.2 | date=2025-02-20 | url=https://www.worldbirdnames.org/new/bow/warblers/ | access-date=2025-11-20}}</ref> with fairly extensive intergradation and mixing where they meet.<ref name="Yoo">{{cite journal | last1=Yoo | first1=Gihyun | last2=Weir | first2=Jason T. | title=Population Divergence in the Eastern North American Boreal Forests: Extensive Gene Flow Characterises a Hybrid Zone Between Palm Warbler Subspecies (Parulidae: Setophaga palmarum ) | journal=Molecular Ecology | volume=34 | issue=16 | date=2025 | issn=0962-1083 | pmid=40678896 | pmc=12329643 | doi=10.1111/mec.70032 | url=https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/mec.70032 | access-date=2025-11-20 | page=}}</ref>
* ''S. p. palmarum'' ("brown palm warbler" or "western palm warbler") inhabits the western two-thirds of the breeding range, and migrates ''southeast'' to winter on the Atlantic coast from Delaware to Florida (with a few on the Gulf coast west to Louisiana). It has much less yellow below, with less colorful streaking, and cold grayish-brown upper parts.
* ''S. p. hypochrysea'' ("yellow palm warbler" or "eastern palm warbler") of the eastern third of the breeding range, and migrates ''southwest'' to winter on the Gulf coast from western Florida to Texas; it migrates later in the fall than its western counterpart.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Palm Warbler {{!}} Audubon Field Guide |url=https://www.audubon.org/field-guide/bird/palm-warbler |access-date=2025-03-09 |website=Audubon |publisher=National Audubon Society |language=en}}</ref> It has brownish-olive upper parts and thoroughly yellow underparts with bold rufous breast and flank streaking. 

==Distribution==
Palm warblers breed in open coniferous [[bog]]s and edge east of the [[Continental Divide]], across [[Canada]] and the northeastern [[United States]].

These birds [[bird migration|migrate]] to the [[southeastern United States]], the [[Yucatán Peninsula]], islands of the [[Caribbean]], and eastern [[Nicaragua]] south to [[Panama]] to winter.<ref>{{cite web |url=https://www.birds.cornell.edu/AllAboutBirds/BirdGuide/Palm_Warbler.html |title=Palm Warbler |work=All About Birds |publisher=[[Cornell Laboratory of Ornithology]] |access-date=2008-10-12}}</ref> They are one of the earlier migrants to return to their breeding grounds in the spring, often completing their migration almost two months before most other warblers. Unlike most ''Setophaga'' species, the palm warbler's winter range includes much of the Atlantic coast of North America, extending as far north as southern Nova Scotia.<ref>{{cite web|title=Palm warbler ''Dendroica palmarum''|url=https://www.mbr-pwrc.usgs.gov/bbs/htm96/cbc622/ra6720.html|publisher=Patuxent Wildlife Research Center|access-date=2017-03-01}}</ref> Every year since 1900 the palm warbler has been observed during [[Christmas Bird Count]] activities in [[Massachusetts]], and consistently since 1958 in [[Nova Scotia]].<ref>{{cite web|title=Results by Species|url=http://netapp.audubon.org/CBCObservation/Historical/ResultsBySpecies.aspx?1|publisher=National Audubon Society|access-date=2017-03-01}}</ref> For the interval 1966–2015 the palm warbler population increased throughout much of its northernmost breeding range.<ref>{{cite web|title=BBS Trend Maps - Palm Warbler ''Setophaga palmarum''|url=https://www.mbr-pwrc.usgs.gov/bbs/tr2015/trend2015_v3.html|publisher=Patuxent Wildlife Research Center|access-date=2018-12-06}}</ref>

The palm warbler has been recorded as a vagrant to [[Iceland]].<ref>Þráinsson, Gunnlaugur (1997) Palm Warbler and Cerulean Warbler in Iceland - new to the Western Palearctic ''[[Birding World]]'' 10(10): 392–393</ref>

== Behavior ==
[[File:Palm Warbler, Homestead, FL 33030, USA imported from iNaturalist photo 62675077.jpg|right|thumb|Eating a [[Halloween pennant]]]]

Palm warbler nests take the form of an open cup, usually situated on or near the ground in an open area.

Palm warblers forage on the ground much more than other warblers, sometimes flying to catch insects. These birds mainly eat insects and berries. Their constant tail bobbing is an identifying characteristic.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Palm Warbler {{!}} Audubon Field Guide |url=https://www.audubon.org/field-guide/bird/palm-warbler |access-date=2023-12-26 |website=www.audubon.org |language=en}}</ref> [[Kirtland's warbler|Kirtland's]], [[prairie warbler|prairie]], and palm warblers are the only [[Setophaga]] species that incessantly bob their tails.

The song of this bird is a monotonous buzzy trill. The call is a sharp ''chek''.<ref>{{cite web | title=Palm Warbler Sounds, All About Birds, Cornell Lab of Ornithology | url=https://www.allaboutbirds.org/guide/Palm_Warbler/sounds# }}</ref>
